speaker: interviewee
text: We just stopped that the guy, Buchner, he came from Treblinka. He escaped. He came back. He was-- his hair was completely white.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he start to talk to us about what he saw with his own eyes. Nobody believe him, particular people who were in charge in the Jewish Council. They were thinking that he got crazy. Impossible or something like that--

speaker: interviewer
text: When I heard him, I realize it that he's telling the truth because was no reason for this man, who extremely intelligent person, educated, to make a story like this up. And we found out one thing, that-- remember that the ghetto, when we came in 1941 in March, they start to make a selection right away, starting in June and repeat quite often every few months. Because supposed to be only 15,000 people who's supposed to be important for the industry, for armament, you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rest, old and children, supposed to go out, but they didn't want to break right away the families. When somebody was working for a German establishment, the family and him came over, but they never allowed them to take anything except five kilo from their home from outside. So they make the people completely paupers. They didn't have enough to eat, and the ghetto was starving, and people has to make a living. Though they were trying to smuggle. Sometime the people got caught. Sometimes they were making razzia in ghetto. Because this ghetto, from 15,000, grew up. People were sneaking in, who didn't have permission to say. So they were making selection, and those people were sent someplace. Supposed to be to the labor camps as a penalty, but they were send-- they sending them to Treblinka or Belsitz or-- or to Chelm or to other places where they were killing them the minute they arrive there. There were single people survive like, the Mr. Buchner one.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you was-- you was working for the Council. There was some ghetto-- ghetto police units. You was working on one of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was there from exactly-- when the Jewish Council transferred to ghetto, and I was working in the Jewish Council as an assistant, the president, before he was arrested-- because he was arrested few months later-- he asked to join it because they needed the police. In this time, I figure out this is a obligation and is a moral duty to protect the Jewish community and ghetto, particular, the Germans was-- we were not under jurisdiction of Gestapo in this time. Only under jurisdiction under Schutzpo. There's two different branches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schutzpolizei.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schutzpolizei, you see? And they said, you will protect the people from the outside world who would like to come over or steal something or something. And I was there from March, maybe 15 or-- because I was still working as a assistant to the president, and you have to join it. And there were about 40 of those policemen, so called.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had some uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have the uniform, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what kind of uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coincidentally, I was using my black pants, the officer pants, because I dyed them black, and-- and I have boots, you see? And we have a jacket, regular like this, closed with the buttons here, and we have the armband here-- the Ordnungsdiest. But strange things-- a couple months later-- And a hat, a special hat. Similar what we saw in the movie. It was exactly this. Exactly what I wore, dress. Some-- not everybody has the same uniform. Sometimes they have their own clothes only with the armband but-- because it was beginning. And the duty was, for example, that we patrol the street and-- and the perimeter of the ghetto. Later on, they build the walls. So was very easy and we-- we stay on the guard on the-- on the gate. But in about in April, about two months later--

speaker: interviewer
text: April which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. When they closed the ghetto, they got information-- the Jewish Council-- that from jurisdiction of Schutzpo, the jurisdiction is coming to the Department Five of Gestapo. This is political department, Jewish department. Then I said to Mila, Mila, this is not anymore job for me. But you cannot quit. You cannot say I don't want to be. So I went to a brother, to the president, Dr. Biberstein, Mark Biberstein was the president. Dr. Alexander Biberstein was a family doctor of ours from before the war. I went to him, and he was a doctor appointed by the Jewish Council to be a doctor for the OD, the Ordnungsdiest. I went to him and said, look. I have to quit. [BACKGROUND NOISE] You are not sick. I said, no. You have to find something that I should quit. I don't want to be in the police. I don't like to be under jurisdiction Gestapo, particular that Gestapo was looking for me when I escape from the prisoner of war. They hear my name and bingo. They-- they have me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you pretend that you have a bad back? Oh, I can pretend because I got a bad back before from a accident what I have. I can pretend. And he taught me how to react to certain movement, you understand? So I report-- instead to duty, I report as I am sick so they cannot put me in the work. So I was walking with a cane and limping, you understand? [BACKGROUND NOISE] And they still didn't want to release me. I ask them to be kick out. No. You have to go to a German doctor, and German doctor can release you. So I talk again with Dr. Biberstein. He told me, when he will put your hands-- leg up straight, jump. It's hurting. When he will bend the knee and push it in, you don't have nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was there, the doctor examine but he couldn't find out what is inflammation or nothing. He couldn't find it. So he start to moving my legs. So every time he's-- stretched legs was lifted up, I yell. Every time [LAUGHS] the leg was bended, I-- no. So he wrote me a letter that I have a serious inflammation of the leg nerves and has to be released from the duty.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? Four months later I was on the list to Auschwitz. And who saved me? I was in the evening 10 o'clock at night going to bed, when two older men, one of them was a student of mine-- came in and say, Polerand, you are on the list to be sent to Auschwitz. Hide any place you want, and we report we didn't found you. So I went to another apartment. There were lots of apartments of my friends in the ghetto, but you didn't have to go on the street to get to those apartments because we make holes between the walls from one building to another, and we can travel all over the ghetto without even going on the street. And I stay with them for a few years until everything cool off. And where was Mila?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mila was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but she-- we have a little room-- kitchen. One only-- kitchen. You see, we got in the-- in the building on Jozefinska 2, on the second floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then-- then, I found a job. I went to the German Arbeitz, a work assigning place, you know? Where was a Colonel Szepesi, Hungarian German, who was wonderful. He was executed later on for helping the Jews by the SS in 1943, end of '43, for helping Jews. And he looks on my ID card and see on the ID card was written, Magister Leopold Pfefferberg, gymnasiallehrer, high school teacher. He looks, and he said to me in German, dast ist kein beruf. This is not a occupation-- gymnasial professor. We don't need any gymnasial professor. So I said, but I am a good mechanic. This is a good profession. He erased this and put [GERMAN] a mechanic, polisher. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: You never heard about in this time about Schindler anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I was still-- every time I could get out, I was in the Schindler's or in the factory or his home till-- almost till the ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How happened that you never went to the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he asked me many times. I always was dreaming that I have to get out. How long I was with Mila, you understand, I was in because I have to protect her. But I figure out when anything happen that we will be separated, I get out. And I could thousand times get out. No problem for me to get out from the ghetto. The day when there was ghetto liquidated, I was trying to get out. But then I found out when the place when I was hiding what was a very good place, the dogs were going with Amon Goeth through the street will smell me out because they smell at about 300 meter away a woman with child, and they kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I stepped out from behind, and I pretend that I didn't see them. And I start to move the bundles while people left, and they were sent to the Plaszow already or to Auschwitz-- because they separated one to go to Plaszow and the one to go to the-- to Auschwitz, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they came close to me-- I still have my boots and my breeches-- I click, report to him military way that I got the order from another officer to clean the road. And they start to laughing because it was a stupid order, but he was a officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have a wonderful article written by American major, Air Force major, what means salute? And it's respect to the superior, and superior will obey what you have to say it to him. And the plot was working very good because I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides, I was watching he, too, what he's doing. When he will go for gun, probably I will go for his throat. I don't know. Because nothing to lose anymore. But I was watching him. They were laughing. He hit me with a right punch and said, verschwindet. This mean, get lost. I salute again, click my heels, turned, and slowly I was going away till the next corner. And then I run when he couldn't see me. [INTERPOSING VOICES] When I will be running, he will shoot me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where Mila? Where is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mila was already-- what happened-- we make arrangement with Dr. Alexandrovich, a dear friend of mine, MD doctor, that he, his wife, and his child, Mila and I will go with the sewers out because he already make arrangement with friends to accepted us, you see? But he went earlier because the-- the-- the segregation start in his area sooner than in ours. In the-- you know, they did section by section. So he went to the sewers, and he was one of the few who really got out early in the morning from the sewers, and they disappear in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in to look for them-- I was thinking that they still there-- and I left Mila in apartment. She was very scared, and I was scared. Everybody was scared in this time, you know? When I come back, when I found out that they already went to sewers and already were killing people in the sewers, you see? So I went for Mila. I came over. There's no Mila there anymore. So I start to looking in the hospital because there were about 60, 70, body laying there. Or maybe she-- she got killed or something. Then somebody told me that they saw Mila going with a group of Madritsch-- because she was working for Madritsch-- to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I decide to hide because I figured out when I got out, I can be much more of help for her than being inside with her. But I couldn't do it. I was lucky I came out with life. I was one of the last one. The group was maybe about 25, 30 people who were supposed to clean something. And after when this group left, ghetto was sealed. Close to 7,000 people was killed over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you saw all this killing around you and all these people, do you believe in God, in the religion, that God will save you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very difficult question to answer. You-- believing in God, this is pray and say I'll hide. I believed more in destiny. I believed that you create your own destiny, you see? And I was looking always for way to get out, not to stay in. I am not a followers. I am a type of a leader. I never follow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I try to be-- when somebody lead me, I thinks I'm as good as him or maybe better, so I always try to be the leader, you see? In any place, anytime, and I select my work the way I think is proper for me in case of emergency. Like I was working the garage-- how I got to garage? I saw a guy with the black glasses in-- in Plaszow working, and I ask the people what the guy is doing. He's a welder. And he can walk around the camp and nobody bother him? No, because maybe he has a job. This is for me. When they were asking me-- they saw me, my kennkarte, that I am mechanic. What is it? What specialty? I am a welder. I never weld in my life. They send me to this guy, and the Gestapo officer from the camp brought me in and said to this guy-- there was Mr. Mandel-- became a good friend of mine after when we met-- who was real welder, a professional for many, many years. He was much older than I.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, this guy is a welder, and I know you need a helper. Try him out. I am going for lunch. I'm coming back in about half an hour, 45 minutes. And then he left. And he's looking at me. Show me your hands. I showed him my hands. You are not a welder. I said, who told you I'm a welder. I told him I'm a welder, not to you. I'm a--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he knew about me little bit that I was a professor. I was a teacher. He said, how I can teach you welding? You show me how you start and what you doing, and you-- I try it. I got a little touch of it. When the guy came in, he said, how he is? He said, oh, yes. He's good, but he need a little practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the guy asked me about the welding. I said, I tell you something. The temperature is 1,200, the hottest point. The other is about 750 Celsius. When you melt it, you have to watch it to melt it the proper way. He didn't ask me to weld it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long you was working in the garage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working till almost to the-- until liquidation of the-- of the-- of the camp. But in between, I was lucky again. They need a welder to weld the-- the cool houses. I mean, the-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Refrigerator.

speaker: interviewee
text: Refrigeration. The two of us they pick up, and we will be the welders and we will be supervised, all the pipes and everything because always something to be done. So for few months, we were there, and this was a part of the refrigeration for German officers. They have their sausages and butter and cheeses.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you find Mila in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were-- every day we saw each other. She was in the women barrack. When she came from work, I see her. Sometimes on the upper plaza, I saw her. I-- I could go to their barracks. She can come to-- to our barrack. Sunday we could-- when we don't work--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the living condition for you wasn't so bad in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say they were good. They were maybe I would say you can tolerate this condition. You have a chance that you have your wife or your mother or your sister or someone to talk with them. Later on, they separate. They put a wire, electric wire between the women, and-- but tooks time, you know, to build it. The Jews were building this for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you heard they're killing a lot of people around on the-- around-- around Plaszow. You wasn't afraid that they will kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: May I tell you something? I didn't think about this. I didn't. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the secret to survive, not to think about?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a secret. You see, you cannot be cowardish, not a coward. Only that you have put your head in your sand. I consider myself-- because I was welder that I could go any place. I put welding some-- some part of the welding machine or something or took some tools, put my glasses on the head. And I was going, oh, something is wrong there in this barrack. And I was looking around the way how eventually in emergency is the way to escape from there, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? You met ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: But was not escape. It was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met Amon Goeth some-- some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost on a daily basis because we were working in his garage. We got three times whipped. First time we got 15 on the back. Why? Because one screw in his car fell out, and he was afraid that there's a sabotage.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second time something else happened, and we got 25. The first time, I fake it out. I put a piece of rubber on my back, and they didn't ask us to put the pants down. The second time, [LAUGHS] they ask us to put the pants down, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the third one, they came on Sunday and they kill a dear friend of mine, his sister-in-law, who was cleaning the cars. He was assigned to clean the car. Some reason, Amon Goeth came over and didn't like it. And they call all of us and he asked Ivan, his chauffeur, Ukrainian fellow, to kill the girl. And in our presence, he killed the girl. And then we got another 15 on the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were a lot of selections in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were lots of-- but garage-- there was about 15 or 14 of us there-- was like a special group, untouchable, because we-- they were a very good mechanic and we have about half a dozen cars to service.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this time, there was a lot of killing on Hujowa Gorka. You heard about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did. We cut out from the roof of our garage a little part in the roof, just that we lifted the flap and we could see just on the level of the Hujowa Gorka, on the hill when they were shooting. I have the photo some of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, and we were watching. Like one day, I-- I saw the German officer was executed there. We didn't know who. I-- I-- I think so that was the-- the-- Bosko, the guy who was under-- wonderful human being. Because when I need something to go in the ghetto or get out, he was in the guardhouse. He gave me a pass, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you was thinking you will survive the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I always was thinking I will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Plaszow, definitely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Positively. But the most important things happen in 1944, in May. There came a group of gypsy women from Hungary what were on their way to Auschwitz, but Auschwitz didn't have a place ready for them. So they hold them in Plaszow. And with friend of mine, who is a doctor, at this time, he was doctor-- he was not even doctor, was a medical student-- Rosenberg, from city of Lodz, a friend of Mila. A story unusual with him. Unusual, almost similar what I did, similar plot. He saw me walking with the-- with the-- glasses. He said that he's welder, and they brought him in. And we said he is a good welder. But later on, he transferred to the medical group, and he was assistant in the medical barracks there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working Sunday, and we got just piece of bread, maybe this size. You know. You were there. You see? And we were going and talking-- we should eat right now the whole piece or maybe leave a little bit for later. And we were working, and we see the gypsy woman yelling to us. So we approached the-- the wire separating the women of us and tried to find out what they want. And they little bit German, little bit Hungarian, you know? Kicsi kenyer. Kisci kenyer. We didn't know what mean Kisci kenyer. Kisci kenyer. Give me a piece of bread. Brot in German. So I broke a piece of bread, and I give it this woman. She doesn't want to take it. So I ask her why you are not taking? Gypsy don't take. Gypsy will tell you something, what is your future. So I said fine. You want a piece of bread, tell me the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: She tooks my hand, and look on there and said, you and a close person to you will survive the war. You'll go over big water and will live happy after. Wonderful. I said, what happened to you tomorrow probably will happen to me after tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then my friend give the other gypsy a piece of bread, and she ask him to tell him. And she is telling him word for word the same what they told me. That we were laughing. Is that funny the same proposition that they survive? He survived with his father. I survive with my-- with Mila. We went over big water. He's in Australia. I am in the United States, and we live happy after. Tell me Poldek, your family-- I don't know if I ask you-- was religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father was conservative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents were orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you was-- you, in the-- in the camp-- I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp we didn't think about this, things of pray. Even maybe subconsciously, as as a child, every time at night, when I went to bed, I said, dear God, keep my parents, and my family healthy. And then I fall asleep-- from a little kid. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And always at camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, too. Even today, too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we coming to-- to you heard that the camp will be liquidated you told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Plaszow was liquidated, and we knew that Schindler is preparing to move his factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was there was a lot of talking about it. Everybody wants to get on the list. I figured out I have no access now to-- even that Schindler many time was asking, I want to get for-- for welder to take me out there. But Amon always said to him, when somebody works for me, cannot work for you. And I was there. And I was figuring that the garage is protected. And when the garage is protected, then I will be here till the end. And when the Russian coming-- we knew the Russian Army are 100 kilometers away, and they start to liquidate it, that some way, some times, and they come, in the right moment, I will get out from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mila was very lucky because she was already in-- in the transport in Stutthof. And one SS man, whom I broke his spirit, pull her out. I don't know if Mila told you about this, you see? And he came over and almost kill me, and I stood-- stood up to him. And he became a friend, and he was giving me always a piece of bread or apple or something for Mila, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were still together. This was in October 1944. Then Mila said, Pol, get us on the list to Schindler. He's your friend. He did so much for you and for-- for the family. Maybe will put us on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the SS man whom I broke, Hans Schreiber, and I ask him to give my name and Mila's name to Mr. Schindler. But Oskar Schindler, on his own, already put our name on the list. And we found out ourselves on the way to Brunnlitz, to the new factory-- But you didn't went directly to Brunnlitz right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Brunnlitz, but not directly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the-- the men and women were separated. 900 men went to Gross-Rosen, and they stopped over there. We were stopped over there, and we were assured that we will-- never will see Oskar Schindler again. But about one week we spent there, and one day they give us a half bread-- half a loaf of bread, to every one of us. And by list, they put us on the train to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the condition in Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross-Rosen? Horrible condition. For the whole week, first of all, we didn't work. When we were-- there were no condition to sleep because in the barrack was about 900 people sleeping in the whole barrack. We didn't sleep. We were sitting between the legs one after another like in a row, and we couldn't sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they changed your outfit there? Your-- your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, They took everything and we got the stripe uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you get some numbers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my number is [NON ENGLISH]. [LAUGHS] It's branded in my mind. It's [NON ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON ENGLISH]. 69006. You see, it's easier for me to say from memory right away the German because this was very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't get it on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They never number us. This was strange, too, because we-- we were not supposed to say there. And when we will stay, we were not supposed to be alive, you see? So they didn't have to give us the number. The number people were getting in Auschwitz, in Gross-Rosen, in Mauthausen, and Dachau only when they were considered that they will last there for few months and do the slave labor, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, when you-- when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, when we found out that we leaving Gross-Rosen, what was a death camp-- you understand what I mean-- we start to breathe now. Where we get-- send us? Probably to Schindler, to Brunnlitz, because there's no sense to throw us from Gross-Rosen to another camp. So we knew we were going to Schindler. And about 24 hours later or two days later-- I don't remember the amount of time what passed by-- we found ourselves over there in Schindler's camp with the food waiting for us, with a place. We slept on the floor because we have to build our wooden bed. What we did it ourself, but we were with him, so we were not afraid anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told us-- I don't-- we start to ask him where is-- he knows Mila. He knows my family. He knows Itzhak Stern, Moszek Bejski, and all those people who work with him, you know? We always were close to him and ask him, Herr Direktor, where are the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Till, one day, maybe four or five days later, he said, I just found out that they are in Auschwitz, and I will try everything to get them out. He sent a beautiful German woman with lots of gifts and lots of money to-- to the Officer Hess, you know, the SS officer in charge, to talk with him. Some things happened between him and her, and-- and they gives and the sausages and liquor and vodka what Schindler sended to him with a note that he will be coming himself to pick up the women. And he came. I don't know the women knew that he was there, but we knew that he went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And few days later, he came in, the women are coming. And six o'clock in November, beginning of November, the gate open of the factory, six o'clock and it was foggy day, you know? We stay on the balcony because the place-- our barracks were upstairs, and the factory was downstairs in the building, the front building. And the factory was a tremendous factory-- and-- like a shadow, you know, in the fog, the women were coming. I couldn't recognize my wife, but she said to me later on that she saw me over there on the balcony.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they came in. They got hot food, and I builded-- because I was again welder-- I could go anyplace I wanted. So I start to bring-- there were window to the latrine, but you couldn't open the window to the latrine because it was against the law. And the latrine was for the women, and the other latrine was for men. So what I did, I put a tremendous amount of iron, steel, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] metal plates, everything possible to make a enter to the window. And we knock out the window, and we were talking when they were sitting on the-- in the latrine, we were talking with our wife. Later on, we have a chance to talk with them on the balconies because they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you was--
